title: Fiona's Doll Adirondack Chair
summary: A doll-sized Adirondack chair from a couple of dollar-store furring strips. All the character of the full-size classic, shrunk down — a great first Adirondack.
description:
This is a miniature Adirondack chair scaled for an 18" doll, built from about $4 of 1x2 and 1x3 furring strips. It captures the whole silhouette of the real thing — the raked stringers, the sloped seat, the fanned back slats, the wide arms — in a project small and cheap enough to be a genuinely low-stakes way to learn how an Adirondack goes together before committing lumber to a full-size one.

The stringers are the one part that needs care, exactly as on the grown-up version: they're the curved/angled side pieces that define the seat slope and back angle, and getting their cut right is what makes the chair sit like an Adirondack rather than a deck chair. Measure carefully and follow the angles; everything after that is just screwing slats to a frame.

Because it's a doll chair carrying no real load, the joinery is relaxed — 1-1/4" screws, or even nails and glue, are plenty. Build the seat (front apron and seat slats on the stringers), then the fanned back, then add the front legs, arm supports, and finally the arms. It's a satisfying afternoon build, an ideal gift alongside a doll, and — as the source says — a fabulous confidence-builder if a full Adirondack chair feels intimidating.

tools.note: For the angled stringer cuts — the one fiddly part.
Predrill the thin furring strips so they don't split.
A doll chair still gets handled — soften the edges.

cutList.note: One end cut to the seat/back angle — the defining cut.

steps.title: Cut the parts
steps.body: From the 8 ft 1x3 (actual 3/4" x 2 1/2") cut two stringers at 13 1/2", one front apron at 10" and two front legs at 9" — 55 1/2" of parts, so one board covers it all. From the two 8 ft 1x2s (actual 3/4" x 1 1/2") cut four seat slats at 10", five back slats at 14", three back supports at 8 1/2", two arm supports at 9 1/2" and two arms at 9 1/2"; put the five 14" back slats and two seat slats on the first board and everything else on the second. The stringers are the one part that needs real care: one end of each gets cut to the seat and back angle, and that angle is what makes the chair sit like an Adirondack instead of a deck chair. Cut one stringer, then trace it onto the second and cut to the line, or clamp the two together and cut them as a pair, so they are identical even if the angle itself is your own judgement — the plan gives the length but not the angle.

steps.title: Attach the front apron
steps.body: Stand the two stringers on edge, parallel and the same way round, and screw the 10" front apron across their front ends with its ends flush to the outside faces. That 10" is the chair's full outside width and it is what squares the seat frame, so check the frame across both diagonals before the second screw goes in. Predrill every hole — these are 3/4" furring strips and a screw driven dry near an end will split them — then add glue and drive 1 1/4" screws or nails. With the apron on, the clear space between the stringers is 8 1/2", which is exactly the length of the back supports later.

steps.title: Attach the seat slats
steps.body: Lay the four 10" seat slats across the stringers with their ends flush to the outside faces, matching the apron, and space them with an even gap — a stack of coins or a couple of paint stirrers used as a spacer keeps every gap the same, which is the whole visual of an Adirondack seat. Work from the front apron toward the back so any leftover space falls at the rear where it is least visible. Predrill and drive one 1 1/4" screw into each stringer at each slat, with a dab of glue; nails and glue are plenty on a chair that carries a doll. Check that the frame is still square once the last slat is down.

steps.title: Build the back
steps.body: Lay the five 14" back slats face down and set the three 8 1/2" back supports across them. The numbers land exactly: five 1 1/2" slats is 7 1/2", and four 1/4" gaps between them makes 8 1/2" — the same as the supports and the same as the clear space between the stringers, so the whole back drops between them. Use a 1/4" spacer between each pair of slats and keep the slat bottoms aligned on a straight edge, since that bottom line is what sits on the seat. Predrill and fasten each slat to each support with a 1 1/4" screw or nail and glue, putting one support low where the back meets the seat, one at the top and one between.

steps.title: Attach the back and the front legs
steps.body: Set the finished back down between the two stringers, resting on the seat and leaning against the angled rear ends of the stringers — that angled cut is what sets the recline, so let the back follow it rather than forcing it upright. Predrill through the outside face of each stringer into the ends of the back supports and drive 1 1/4" screws with glue. Then attach the two 9" front legs to the outside faces of the stringers at the front, with the apron between them, so they carry the front of the seat and give the arms something to land on. Stand the chair on a flat surface and check that it does not rock before the glue sets.

steps.title: Attach the arm supports
steps.body: Fasten the two 9 1/2" arm supports so each runs from its front leg back to the back assembly, tying the front and rear of the chair into one unit — this is what stops the back from flexing and what holds the arms level. Set both supports to the same height off the seat by measuring each from the seat slats rather than from the floor, and clamp before fastening so nothing shifts as the screw pulls. Predrill, glue and drive 1 1/4" screws at each end. Sight across the two supports from the front; if they are not level with each other, the arms will not be either.

steps.title: Attach the arms and finish
steps.body: Lay the two 9 1/2" arms on top of the arm supports, overhanging the front leg slightly, and fasten each down with glue and two 1 1/4" screws or nails into the support, predrilling first. Sit a doll in it and check both arms rest at the same height before the glue cures. Then sand every surface and round the arm ends, the front edge of the seat and every corner well — a doll chair gets handled far more than it gets sat on, and a splinter on a toy is the one defect that matters. Finish it if you like, or leave it bare.
